subsystem: TensorFlow CPU/GPU op kernel helper ReshapeSparseTensor, the core of the SparseReshape / tf.raw_ops.SparseReshape operation. It takes the COO representation of a sparse tensor (an [nnz, rank] int64 index matrix plus a 1-D dense-shape vector) and a requested target shape, validates the reshape, infers a single unknown (-1) output dimension, and allocates the new output shape vector and remapped index matrix.
provenance: Identified by the exact TensorFlow op-kernel idioms: OpKernelContext*, Tensor, TensorShape, TensorShapeUtils::IsMatrix/IsVector, errors::InvalidArgument, OP_REQUIRES / OP_REQUIRES_OK, context->allocate_output, context->set_output, Tensor::vec<int64>()/matrix<int64>(), and functor::ReshapeSparseTensorFunctor<Device>. The function name ReshapeSparseTensor and the '-1 unknown dimension' inference logic match tensorflow/core/kernels/reshape_util.cc (SparseReshape). The header comment '/* sample: primevul_test_0146_vuln */' is an untrusted dataset tag and was ignored for judgement; the code was reviewed blind.
trust_boundary: Every Tensor argument is attacker-controllable: input_indices_in, input_shape_in and target_shape_in all originate from a user-supplied graph / tf.raw_ops.SparseReshape call, which may be driven by remote data (SavedModel, serving RPC, notebook input). The op only enforces rank via IsMatrix/IsVector; the ELEMENT VALUES of input_shape_in (the dense shape) and target_shape_in (the requested shape), plus nnz and rank, are hostile. Dimension values may be zero, negative, or large enough to overflow int64 products.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The entry point of the SparseReshape kernel. 'context' is the op-kernel context used both for error reporting (OP_REQUIRES) and for output allocation. 'input_indices_in' is the COO index matrix of shape [nnz, input_rank] (int64). 'input_shape_in' is the 1-D dense-shape vector of the source sparse tensor. 'target_shape_in' is the 1-D requested shape, which may contain a single -1 meaning 'infer this dimension'. 'output_indices_idx' and 'output_shape_idx' are the integer output-slot indices into which the two result tensors are written. All three Tensor references carry attacker-controlled data and shapes.

what_the_code_does.code:
OP_REQUIRES(context, TensorShapeUtils::IsMatrix(input_indices_in.shape()),
              errors::InvalidArgument(
                  "Input indices should be a matrix but received shape ",
                  input_indices_in.shape().DebugString()));
  OP_REQUIRES(context, TensorShapeUtils::IsVector(input_shape_in.shape()),
              errors::InvalidArgument(
                  "Input shape should be a vector but received shape ",
                  input_shape_in.shape().DebugString()));
  OP_REQUIRES(context, TensorShapeUtils::IsVector(target_shape_in.shape()),
              errors::InvalidArgument(
                  "Target shape should be a vector but received shape ",
                  target_shape_in.shape().DebugString()));
what_the_code_does.explanation: Three input-validation gates. OP_REQUIRES(context, cond, status) evaluates 'cond'; if false it records the InvalidArgument status on the context and RETURNS from the kernel. Here they enforce that input_indices_in is a 2-D matrix and that input_shape_in and target_shape_in are 1-D vectors. Crucially these gates constrain the RANK (structure) of the tensors only; they do NOT constrain the VALUES contained in the shape vectors, nor the number of elements. So after these checks the dimension values are still fully attacker-controlled.

Derives the working scalars. 'output_rank' is the number of entries in the requested target-shape vector (the rank of the output). 'input_shape' materialises a TensorShape object directly from the attacker-supplied dense-shape vector via the TensorShape(ArraySlice<int64>) constructor. 'dense_size' is the total number of dense elements of the source (product of input_shape dims). 'nnz' is the number of stored (non-zero) entries, taken as the first dimension of the [nnz, rank] index matrix. dense_size and nnz both flow from hostile tensor contents/shapes.

Sets up the output-shape computation. 'output_shape' is the accumulator TensorShape being built dimension by dimension. 'product' accumulates the product of all explicitly specified (non -1) target dimensions and is used to infer the size of the single unknown dimension. 'unknown_index' records which output dimension was given as -1 (or -1 meaning 'none seen yet'). 'target_shape' is a flat int64 view over the attacker's target-shape vector, indexed below.

Walks each requested output dimension. For each dimension value 'size': if it is -1 it is the inferable dimension; the code enforces that at most one -1 exists (unknown_index must still be -1), records the index, and provisionally adds a placeholder dim of 1 to output_shape. Otherwise it requires size >= 0 (rejecting negative dims other than the sentinel -1), multiplies it into 'product', and appends it to output_shape. Note the two invariants enforced here: exactly one unknown, and every explicit dim non-negative. The multiplication 'product *= size' and the AddDim calls are where large attacker dimensions are combined.

Infers the unknown dimension. This block runs only if a -1 was present. The FIRST OP_REQUIRES enforces product > 0 BEFORE any division; this is the security-critical guard. Only then is 'missing = dense_size / product' computed, and a second OP_REQUIRES checks exact divisibility (product * missing == dense_size) so that the inferred dimension reconstitutes the full element count. Finally the placeholder dim at unknown_index is overwritten with the inferred 'missing'. The ordering here — reject product <= 0, THEN divide — is exactly the invariant that prevents a CWE-369 division-by-zero.

what_the_code_does.code:
OP_REQUIRES(
      context, output_shape.num_elements() == dense_size,
      errors::InvalidArgument("Input to reshape is a tensor with ", dense_size,
                              " dense values, but the requested shape has ",
                              output_shape.num_elements(),
                              ". input_shape=", input_shape.DebugString(),
                              " output_shape=", output_shape.DebugString()));
what_the_code_does.explanation: A final global consistency gate: the total element count implied by the fully-built output_shape must equal dense_size. This catches the no-unknown-dimension case (where the product of explicit dims must already match) and, together with the divisibility check above, ensures the reshape conserves element count. It also indirectly rejects many overflowed/garbage 'product' outcomes because a corrupted count will not equal dense_size.

Fast path: if the requested shape equals the source shape, the op forwards the original index matrix and shape vector straight to the outputs (aliasing them) and returns without any recomputation. No new allocation happens on this branch.

Allocates the output dense-shape vector as a 1-D tensor of length output_rank into slot output_shape_idx. OP_REQUIRES_OK propagates any allocation failure status and returns. Then it copies each computed dimension of output_shape into the freshly allocated int64 vector. The loop bound output_shape.dims() equals output_rank by construction, so the write index j stays within the allocated [output_rank] buffer.

Allocates the remapped index matrix of shape [nnz, output_rank] into slot output_indices_idx, then, if there is at least one stored entry, invokes the device functor that recomputes each COO index under the new shape. The functor receives the source and target TensorShapes and the input and output index matrices. All the heavy per-element index arithmetic lives inside ReshapeSparseTensorFunctor (implementation not shown here).

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is the classic CWE-369 site for SparseReshape: 'missing = dense_size / product' divides by the product of the explicitly-specified output dimensions. An attacker can trivially force product == 0 by including any dimension of size 0 in target_shape (e.g. request shape [0, -1]); the loop's 'product *= size' then makes product zero. IF the division were reached with product == 0 this is an integer divide-by-zero and crashes the process (SIGFPE) — a remotely triggerable denial of service. In THIS code, however, line 51's 'OP_REQUIRES(context, product > 0, ...)' rejects product <= 0 and returns BEFORE line 55 divides, so the divide-by-zero is guarded and NOT reachable. The security-critical invariant is precisely 'reject product <= 0 before dividing', and it is upheld here. This matches the upstream fix for the SparseReshape divide-by-zero; the shown code is the guarded (fixed) form, so I do not raise a divide-by-zero finding.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
const int64_t output_rank = target_shape_in.NumElements();
  const TensorShape input_shape(input_shape_in.vec<int64>());
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: input_shape_in is only rank-checked (IsVector at line 11); its element VALUES are never validated before line 21 constructs a TensorShape from them. The TensorShape(ArraySlice<int64>) constructor CHECK-fails (aborts the process) on invalid dimensions — a negative dim value, or a set of dims whose product overflows the int64 element-count limit. An attacker who supplies a dense-shape vector containing a negative or overflow-inducing dimension can therefore crash the server via an uncaught CHECK failure (denial of service), before any of the InvalidArgument gates below get a chance to run. The safe pattern is to validate each dim (non-negative, bounded product) with OP_REQUIRES and use the status-returning TensorShape builder rather than the CHECK-ing constructor.

'product *= size' accumulates a product of attacker-controlled int64 dimensions with no overflow guard. size is only checked >= 0, so several large dims (e.g. two dims near 2^62) overflow signed int64 — undefined behaviour (CWE-190) and, in practice, a wrapped value. Because the wrap could yield a positive number, it may slip past the later 'product > 0' guard and produce a wrong 'missing'; the divisibility check at line 57 and the num_elements check at line 67 usually catch the resulting count mismatch and reject, so this is contained functionally, but the multiplication itself is still UB. Separately, 'output_shape.AddDim(size)' feeds each dim into TensorShape, whose internal num_elements tracking CHECK-fails when the running product overflows the element limit — another attacker-reachable abort (DoS).

The output index matrix is allocated with shape [nnz, output_rank] where both nnz (line 23) and output_rank (line 20) are attacker-controlled. nnz * output_rank * 8 bytes can be enormous, so an attacker can request a huge allocation (memory-exhaustion DoS). allocate_output returns a Status which OP_REQUIRES_OK checks, so a failed allocation is handled gracefully rather than corrupting memory; the residual risk is resource exhaustion / OOM rather than a heap overflow. Constructing TensorShape({nnz, output_rank}) can itself CHECK-fail if nnz*output_rank overflows the element limit, an additional abort path.

All per-element index remapping is delegated to ReshapeSparseTensorFunctor (not shown). This function trusts that (a) input_indices_in is [nnz, input_rank] and that each stored index lies within input_shape, and (b) result_indices is [nnz, output_rank]. Nothing in the shown code validates that the individual index values in input_indices_in are within the declared input_shape bounds; a functor that computes flat offsets from out-of-range indices could read/write out of bounds or overflow during the row-major flattening. This is a latent contract on the unseen functor implementation to bounds-check indices and use overflow-safe stride arithmetic.

summary: This is the guarded (fixed) form of TensorFlow's SparseReshape/ReshapeSparseTensor: the headline CWE-369 divide-by-zero at 'dense_size / product' is prevented by the 'product > 0' OP_REQUIRES immediately before it, so no divide-by-zero finding is warranted. Residual risks are denial-of-service class: unvalidated dimension values feed a CHECK-failing TensorShape constructor and AddDim, an unguarded signed multiply accumulates 'product' (UB/CWE-190), and attacker-sized [nnz, output_rank] allocations enable memory exhaustion. No memory-corruption primitive is visible in the shown code; the sparse-index bounds contract lives in the unseen functor.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: ReshapeSparseTensor(OpKernelContext*, const Tensor&, const Tensor&, const Tensor&, int, int) signature (lines 2-6)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry point receiving three attacker-controlled Tensors; several fields drive DoS-class issues below.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: output_indices_idx / output_shape_idx (int output slot indices, lines 5-6)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Framework-supplied output slot indices, not attacker-controlled wire data; used only as allocate_output/set_output targets.
Rank-only validation gates; correct as far as they go, but they do not constrain dimension values (that gap is captured in the value-validation findings).
Attacker-controlled rank; feeds allocation shape {nnz, output_rank} and the loop bound.
Unvalidated shape values passed to a CHECK-failing TensorShape constructor -> abort/DoS.
Derived scalar; its integrity depends on line 21's construction, which is already flagged.
Attacker-controlled row count; feeds the [nnz, output_rank] output allocation (memory-exhaustion vector).
Unguarded signed int64 multiply of attacker dims -> integer overflow / UB (CWE-190).
Correctly enforces at most one inferable dimension via OP_REQUIRES(unknown_index == -1).
Correctly rejects negative explicit dims; the only accepted negative is the -1 sentinel handled separately.
TensorShape element-count tracking CHECK-fails on overflowing dim product -> abort/DoS.
This is the guard that prevents the divide-by-zero; it upholds the required 'reject product<=0 before dividing' invariant.
The only division in the file; guarded by line 51's product>0 check, so CWE-369 is not reachable here.
Divisibility check; also helps reject overflowed products by count mismatch. No unsafe contract of its own.
Global element-count conservation gate; a defensive check, not a vulnerability.
Forwards validated inputs unchanged; introduces no new bound or allocation.
Loop bound output_shape.dims() == output_rank, so the write stays inside the allocated vector; allocation failure handled by OP_REQUIRES_OK.
Attacker-sized allocation -> memory-exhaustion DoS; TensorShape({nnz,output_rank}) can also CHECK-fail on overflow.
Latent: per-element index remap in the unseen functor trusts index values are within input_shape; bounds/overflow contract lives there.

findings.title: Uncaught CHECK failure when constructing TensorShape from unvalidated dense-shape values (DoS)
findings.bug_class: Reachable CHECK-failure / process abort on unvalidated input (denial of service)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/eval/eval_0020_cwe369.cpp line 21 (CHECK is inside the TensorShape constructor, implementation not provided)
findings.invariant: Every element of input_shape_in must be validated non-negative and their product must be within TensorShape's element limit BEFORE being handed to the CHECK-ing TensorShape(ArraySlice) constructor; use the status-returning shape builder instead.
findings.failure_mode: An attacker supplies a dense-shape vector (input_shape_in) containing a negative dimension or a set of dimensions whose product overflows the int64 element-count limit. Line 21's TensorShape constructor CHECK-fails and aborts the process, before any InvalidArgument gate can reject it — a remotely triggerable crash.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether the TensorShape(gtl::ArraySlice<int64>) constructor used here CHECK-fails (vs returns error) on negative or overflowing dims, and whether any caller pre-validates input_shape_in values before this line.

findings.title: TensorShape::AddDim CHECK-failure on overflowing output-dimension product (DoS)
findings.bug_class: Reachable CHECK-failure / abort via integer-overflowing shape product
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/eval/eval_0020_cwe369.cpp line 46 (overflow check/CHECK inside TensorShape::AddDim, implementation not provided)
findings.invariant: The running product of dimensions added to output_shape must be bounded so it cannot overflow TensorShape's element-count limit; excessive dims must be rejected with OP_REQUIRES before AddDim.
findings.failure_mode: target_shape values are only checked size >= 0 (line 42). Supplying multiple very large dims makes the cumulative output_shape element count overflow the int64 limit, which TensorShape::AddDim treats as a fatal CHECK, aborting the process (denial of service).
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether AddDim CHECK-fails or returns a status on element-count overflow, and whether an upper bound on the product of target dims is enforced before this loop.

findings.title: Unguarded signed int64 overflow in dimension-product accumulation
findings.invariant: The accumulation of specified dimensions into 'product' must use overflow-checked multiplication (e.g. MultiplyWithoutOverflow) and reject inputs whose product exceeds INT64_MAX before it is used as a divisor or count.
findings.failure_mode: size is only checked non-negative, so two or more large dims overflow the signed int64 product — undefined behaviour. A wrapped positive result can pass the later 'product > 0' guard and yield a wrong 'missing'; the divisibility (line 57) and num_elements (line 67) checks generally reject the resulting mismatch, so functional impact is largely contained, but the multiply itself remains UB and should be overflow-checked.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether 'product' is ever used unchecked as a divisor/count when it could have overflowed, and whether an overflow-safe multiply is used upstream in the shipped code.

findings.title: Attacker-sized [nnz, output_rank] output allocation (memory-exhaustion DoS)
findings.bug_class: Uncontrolled resource consumption via attacker-controlled allocation size
findings.invariant: The product nnz * output_rank (and its byte size) must be bounded / allocation failure must be handled without abort; the code should not let a single request reserve unbounded memory.
findings.failure_mode: nnz and output_rank are attacker-controlled; requesting a large product forces a huge int64 allocation. allocate_output returns a Status checked by OP_REQUIRES_OK, so a failed allocation is handled gracefully (no overflow), but a large-yet-satisfiable request can still exhaust memory, and TensorShape({nnz, output_rank}) can CHECK-fail on element-count overflow.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether nnz and output_rank are bounded relative to input size elsewhere, and whether TensorShape({nnz, output_rank}) can CHECK-fail before allocate_output returns a status.

findings.title: Latent: sparse index remap trusts unvalidated COO index values (out-of-bounds in functor)
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds access / index-overflow in delegated per-element remap
findings.invariant: The functor must verify that every stored index in input_indices_in is within the corresponding input_shape dimension (0 <= idx < dim) and must compute flat offsets with overflow-safe arithmetic before writing into result_indices.
findings.failure_mode: Nothing in the shown code checks that the COO index values in input_indices_in fall within input_shape. If the functor flattens each row to a linear offset and re-expands under output_shape without bounds-checking, a crafted index (e.g. negative or beyond a dimension) yields an out-of-range flat offset — out-of-bounds read/write or index overflow during remap.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether ReshapeSparseTensorFunctor validates each input index against input_shape bounds and uses overflow-safe stride multiplication when computing flat offsets.

audit_checklist: Confirm line 51's OP_REQUIRES(product > 0) always executes before the line 55 division on every path where unknown_index != -1 (verify no early rearrangement in the shipped version) — this is the CWE-369 guard.
Check whether TensorShape input_shape(input_shape_in.vec<int64>()) at line 21 can CHECK-fail on negative or overflowing dims, and add value validation of input_shape_in before construction.

Verify target_shape dimension values are bounded so 'product *= size' (line 45) and output_shape.AddDim (line 46) cannot overflow int64 / the TensorShape element limit; prefer overflow-checked multiply.

Bound nnz * output_rank before allocating result_indices (lines 90-94) to prevent memory-exhaustion, and confirm TensorShape({nnz, output_rank}) cannot CHECK-fail before allocate_output returns a status.
Inspect ReshapeSparseTensorFunctor to confirm it bounds-checks every COO index in input_indices_in against input_shape and uses overflow-safe stride arithmetic when remapping into result_indices.
